title: Cisco AI Endpoint Behavioural (AEB) Profile-Drift and Behaviour-Anomaly Detection
dataSources: Cisco AEB events sourced via Catalyst Center → ISE pxGrid integration. Behaviour anomaly events appear under *AI Endpoint Behaviour* category in ISE syslog — codes `80200` (behaviour baseline established), `80201` (behaviour anomaly detected), `80202` (behaviour profile drift). Field: `endpoint_mac`, `anomaly_type`, `anomaly_score`.
spl:
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (80201, 80202)
| stats count by endpoint_mac, anomaly_type
| where count > 0
description: Detects endpoint behaviour-pattern anomalies (traffic class drift, time-of-use anomaly, peer-set change) reported by Cisco AI Endpoint Behavioural analytics.
value: Behaviour anomalies are hard to detect manually. AEB models identify when an endpoint suddenly starts behaving differently — often the earliest sign of compromise. Surfacing these in Splunk feeds the SOC's risk model.
visualization: Top-N anomaly types; per-endpoint anomaly history.

references.title: Cisco AI Endpoint Analytics — Behavioural Insights

knownFalsePositives:
(a) Holiday / off-hours pattern shifts.
(b) New software/job role changing endpoint behaviour.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Simulate behaviour change on an endpoint (e.g. abrupt traffic-pattern shift). AEB emits 80201. UC-17.1.67 fires.

grandmaExplanation: ISE notices if a device suddenly starts acting unusually — like a printer that suddenly tries to talk to email servers. We surface these unusual changes for the security team to investigate.
